title: Statistics Basics — Causal Inference, Information Theory, and the Replication Crisis
key_insight: The replication crisis (2011-present) revealed that ~50% of published psychology and medical studies don't replicate. The root causes: p-hacking, underpowered studies, publication bias, HARKing. Understanding these forces you to apply statistics rigorously.
body:
## Causal Inference Beyond Correlation

```python
# Pearl's Causal Hierarchy:
# Level 1: Association — P(Y | X=x) — what I observe
# Level 2: Intervention — P(Y | do(X=x)) — what happens if I force X
# Level 3: Counterfactual — P(Y_X=x | X=x', Y=y') — what would have happened

# The do() operator separates correlation from causation
# Correlation: P(cancer | smoking) — could be confounded (smokers differ)
# Causation:   P(cancer | do(smoking)) — if we force-assigned smoking

# Regression Discontinuity Design (natural experiment):
# Students with test score >= 70 get a scholarship
# Compare students just above/below cutoff — essentially random
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
n = 1000
scores = np.random.normal(70, 10, n)
# True treatment effect: scholarship → 5 point income boost (in thousands)
treatment = scores >= 70
income = 30 + 0.5 * scores + 5 * treatment + np.random.normal(0, 3, n)

# Local linear regression near cutoff:
bandwidth = 5
mask = np.abs(scores - 70) <= bandwidth
below = income[mask & ~treatment].mean()
above = income[mask & treatment].mean()
print(f'RD Estimate of treatment effect: {above - below:.2f}')  # ~5.0
```

## Information Theory: Entropy and KL Divergence

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.special import rel_entr

# Shannon entropy: H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x)
# Measures: average uncertainty / information content

def entropy(probs):
    probs = np.array(probs)
    return -np.sum(probs * np.log2(probs + 1e-10))

print(entropy([0.5, 0.5]))      # 1.0 bit — maximum uncertainty (fair coin)
print(entropy([0.99, 0.01]))    # 0.08 bits — very certain
print(entropy([0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]))  # 2.0 bits

# KL Divergence: D_KL(P||Q) = Σ p(x) log(p(x)/q(x))
# Measures how different distribution Q is from P
# Used in VAEs, knowledge distillation, information gain

P = np.array([0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1])
Q = np.array([0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25])

kl = np.sum(rel_entr(P, Q))  # scipy's rel_entr handles 0s
print(f'KL(P||Q): {kl:.4f}')  # how much more you need to describe P using Q's code

# Cross-entropy (used as loss in neural networks):
# H(P, Q) = H(P) + KL(P||Q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x)
cross_entropy = -np.sum(P * np.log(Q))
print(f'Cross-entropy: {cross_entropy:.4f}')
```

## The Replication Crisis: Statistical Roots

```python
# P-hacking simulation: demonstrates how easy it is to get p < 0.05 by chance
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

def simulate_p_hacking(n_experiments=10000, sample_size=30, n_metrics=10):
    """Each experiment measures 10 metrics. Report the best p-value."""
    false_positives = 0
    for _ in range(n_experiments):
        # True null: A and B are identical
        A = np.random.normal(0, 1, sample_size)
        B = np.random.normal(0, 1, sample_size)
        
        # Test 10 different metrics
        p_values = [stats.ttest_ind(A + np.random.normal(0, 0.1, sample_size),
                                     B + np.random.normal(0, 0.1, sample_size))[1]
                    for _ in range(n_metrics)]
        
        # Report the minimum p-value (cherry picking)
        if min(p_values) < 0.05:
            false_positives += 1
    
    return false_positives / n_experiments

false_positive_rate = simulate_p_hacking()
print(f'False positive rate with 10 metrics: {false_positive_rate:.2%}')
# ~40% — testing 10 metrics, reporting only the best = massive false positive rate

# Fixes:
# 1. Pre-register your hypothesis BEFORE data collection
# 2. Use Bonferroni correction: α_corrected = 0.05 / n_tests
# 3. Report all tests, not just significant ones
```
common_mistakes: Treating KL divergence as symmetric — KL(P||Q) ≠ KL(Q||P). This matters for variational inference: using the wrong direction minimises different things
Ignoring publication bias when reading meta-analyses — studies with p > 0.05 rarely get published, so meta-analyses systematically overestimate effect sizes
mini_exercise: Re-analyse a published study in your field using the replication crisis checklist: (1) Was hypothesis pre-registered? (2) What is the sample size and power? (3) How many hypotheses were tested? (4) What is the effect size (Cohen's d)? (5) Could the result be noise given the multiple comparisons?
